mnemonics: Dynamic Hashing: Directories Expand And Contract (DEAC) to handle growth, like a file cabinet adding new drawers.
Priority Queue: Heap It Up! (A heap is often used to implement an efficient priority queue, and you 'heap' items in order of priority.)
keyPoints: Dynamic Hashing (Extendible Hashing) uses a directory (like an index) to point to data buckets, allowing for efficient expansion and contraction as data is added or removed.
The priority of an element in a Priority Queue determines the order of its removal (e.g., highest priority first or lowest priority first).
A Binary Heap is a common, efficient data structure used to implement a Priority Queue, supporting insertions and removal of the highest/lowest priority element in O(log n) time.
commonMistakes: Confusing a Priority Queue with a regular FIFO Queue; a Priority Queue removes items based on priority, not the order of insertion.
Incorrectly updating priorities in a heap-based Priority Queue without reheapifying, which can break the heap property and lead to incorrect results.
Assuming Dynamic Hashing (Extendible) is the same as Static Hashing; the key difference is the dynamic directory that allows for on-the-fly resizing without rehashing all existing keys.
